title: Camera Settings Cheat Sheet for Every Astro Subject
excerpt: Stop guessing in the dark. Here are the exact camera settings for every astrophotography subject, from the Milky Way to the Sun.
author: Ace Stellar
body:
## One Cheat Sheet to Rule Them All

Nothing kills a night of astrophotography faster than fumbling with settings in the dark while the Milky Way core drifts behind a cloud. This cheat sheet gives you tested starting points for every major astro subject. Bookmark it, screenshot it, tattoo it on your forearm — whatever works.

These settings assume a full-frame camera. If you're shooting crop sensor (APS-C), divide the focal length by your crop factor (1.5x for Nikon/Sony, 1.6x for Canon) when calculating max exposure time, and you may need to bump ISO slightly.

## Milky Way (Untracked)

- **Lens:** 14-24mm, f/2.8 or wider
- **Aperture:** Wide open (f/1.4, f/1.8, f/2.8)
- **Shutter speed:** 500 / focal length (e.g., 500/14 = ~35s, but 20-25s is safer)
- **ISO:** 3200-6400
- **Focus:** Manual, on a bright star
- **White balance:** ~3800-4200K

**Why these settings?** You're fighting two enemies: Earth's rotation (which blurs stars in long exposures) and darkness (which demands wide apertures and high ISO). The wide aperture lets in maximum light. The 500 Rule keeps stars as points rather than trails. High ISO amplifies the faint light from the galactic core.

## Milky Way (Star Tracker)

- **Lens:** 35-85mm, f/2.8 or wider
- **Aperture:** f/2.8-4 (can stop down slightly for sharper corners)
- **Shutter speed:** 60-120 seconds
- **ISO:** 800-1600
- **Focus:** Manual, on a bright star
- **White balance:** ~3800-4200K

**Why these settings?** A star tracker compensates for Earth's rotation, so you can expose much longer without star trails. This means lower ISO (less noise) and the option to use longer focal lengths for detailed Milky Way close-ups. The tradeoff: your foreground will be blurred if you don't shoot it separately.

## Moon (Crescent to Full)

- **Lens:** 200mm+ (longer is better, 500mm+ is ideal)
- **Aperture:** f/8-f/11
- **Shutter speed:** 1/125 - 1/250 (full Moon), 1/60 - 1/125 (quarter), 1/30 (crescent)
- **ISO:** 100-400
- **Focus:** Manual or AF on the Moon's surface
- **White balance:** Daylight (~5500K)

**Why these settings?** The Moon is shockingly bright — it's reflecting direct sunlight. The classic "Looney 11" rule says: at f/11, your shutter speed is 1/ISO. So at ISO 100, f/11, you'd shoot 1/100s. This prevents the Moon from being a blown-out white blob. Stopping down to f/8-11 also gives you sharp lens performance.

## Planets (Jupiter, Saturn, Mars)

- **Setup:** Telescope (1000mm+) with camera adapter, or 500mm+ lens with 2x teleconverter
- **Aperture:** Determined by telescope (typically f/10-f/12)
- **Shutter speed:** 1/30 - 1/250 (video mode preferred)
- **ISO:** 200-800
- **Focus:** Manual, use Live View 10x zoom
- **Special:** Shoot video (60fps+), then stack best frames in software like AutoStakkert

**Why these settings?** Planetary imaging is all about beating atmospheric turbulence ("seeing"). Short exposures freeze the wobble. By shooting video and stacking the sharpest 10-20% of frames, you average out the turbulence and reveal stunning detail — cloud bands on Jupiter, rings of Saturn, polar caps on Mars.

## Deep Sky Objects (Galaxies, Nebulae)

- **Setup:** Telescope or telephoto lens on a tracking mount
- **Aperture:** f/4-f/7 (telescope dependent)
- **Shutter speed:** 60-300 seconds per sub-frame
- **ISO:** 800-1600 (or Unity Gain for astro cameras)
- **Focus:** Bahtinov mask or manual with Live View zoom
- **Frames:** 50-200+ light frames, plus darks, flats, and bias calibration frames

**Why these settings?** Deep sky objects are incredibly faint. You need long total integration time (hours) to pull signal out of the noise. A tracking mount follows the sky's rotation precisely. You break the total exposure into many sub-frames and stack them — this averages out noise while preserving signal. Calibration frames remove sensor artifacts.

## Star Trails

- **Lens:** 14-35mm (wider = longer trails per frame)
- **Aperture:** f/2.8-f/4
- **Shutter speed:** 30 seconds per frame, shoot 100-300+ consecutive frames
- **ISO:** 800-1600
- **Focus:** Manual, on a bright star
- **Special:** Use an intervalometer with 1-second gap between frames. Stack in StarStaX or Sequator.

**Why these settings?** Star trail photography is actually many short exposures stacked together, not one mega-long exposure. This prevents sensor heating noise, lets you create timelapse videos from the same frames, and means one airplane or headlight only ruins one frame (which you can delete) instead of your entire shot. Point north to capture circular trails around Polaris.

## Solar Photography

- **MANDATORY:** White-light solar filter or dedicated H-alpha telescope. NEVER point an unfiltered camera or telescope at the Sun.
- **Lens:** 200mm+ with solar filter
- **Aperture:** f/8-f/11
- **Shutter speed:** 1/500 - 1/2000
- **ISO:** 100-200
- **Focus:** Manual or AF on sunspot detail
- **Special:** Shoot video for stacking, just like planetary

**Why these settings?** With a proper solar filter (which blocks 99.999% of light), the Sun becomes a manageable subject. Fast shutter speeds freeze atmospheric shimmer. Low ISO keeps noise minimal. Stacking video frames sharpens the result dramatically. You can capture sunspots, granulation, and prominences (with H-alpha).

## Quick Reference Table

| Subject | Focal Length | Aperture | Shutter | ISO | Notes |
|---------|-------------|----------|---------|-----|-------|
| Milky Way | 14-24mm | f/1.4-2.8 | 15-25s | 3200-6400 | 500 Rule |
| MW Tracked | 35-85mm | f/2.8-4 | 60-120s | 800-1600 | Separate foreground |
| Moon | 200mm+ | f/8-11 | 1/125-1/250 | 100-400 | Looney 11 Rule |
| Planets | 1000mm+ | f/10-12 | Video 60fps | 200-800 | Stack best frames |
| Deep Sky | 200mm+ | f/4-7 | 60-300s | 800-1600 | Needs tracker + stacking |
| Star Trails | 14-35mm | f/2.8-4 | 30s x 100+ | 800-1600 | Stack in StarStaX |
| Sun | 200mm+ | f/8-11 | 1/500-1/2000 | 100-200 | MUST use solar filter |

## The Settings That Never Change

No matter what you're shooting:

- **File format:** RAW. Always. No exceptions.
- **Image stabilization:** OFF when on a tripod.
- **Long exposure noise reduction:** OFF (you'll use calibration frames or stacking instead).
- **LCD brightness:** Minimum (so you don't blow out your night vision and misjudge exposure).
- **Mirror lockup / EFCS:** ON when available (reduces vibration).
- **Back-button focus:** Highly recommended (separates focus from the shutter button so you don't accidentally refocus).

Print this out, laminate it, and toss it in your camera bag. When it's 2 AM and 30 degrees and you can't remember if the Moon needs ISO 100 or ISO 6400, you'll thank yourself.
